title: AI sway: unions steer ALP
full_text:
Labor’s draft policy platform has been significantly amended to insert a raft of union priorities for regulating artificial intelligence, tying Anthony Albanese to a more heavy-handed oversight over the technology’s rollout after this month’s national conference.
As the Prime Minister moves to use the 50th national conference to project strength and unity going into the next election against both One Nation and the Coalition, the final draft to be debated in Adelaide also includes more extensive criticisms of Israel’s “occupation” of Gaza while codifying contentious tax hikes in the May budget into the party’s formal agenda.
Labor has also backflipped on an earlier decision to dump the reference to the Albanese government’s 82 per cent renewables target in its policy platform, with the goal being explicitly inserted into the final draft after The Australian revealed in April it was omitted from an earlier version.
The document, which is supposed to bind Labor governments, commits the Albanese government to considering a new AI Expert Panel within the Fair Work Commission, reviewing whether anti-discrimination laws are fit for purpose in dealing with AI and ensuring there is human oversight and accountability for decisions made by AI in the public service.
It also endorses a process of “co-design” between businesses and workers on AI implementation, an approach business groups oppose because they argue it could give unions an effective veto over technology uptake.
“Labor will consider what steps can be taken to protect workers, in the face of changing technologies and the introduction of AI,” the document says. “Labor will ensure that the adoption of AI safeguards worker and community interests, especially in our creative, media and other industries reliant on intellectual property.” The new platform provisions on AI are at odds with the initial “light touch” framework released by the government last year, after business issued concerns Labor would use the industrial relations system to regulate the rollout of the technology. But the codified commitment to protect intellectual property rights will be welcomed by the media industry, amid concerns the government was considering backflipping on its promise to protect creatives as it comes under pressure from AI giants to water down copyright laws.
The draft national platform, which will be voted on by delegates – MPs, unionists and branch members – explicitly opposes the “annexation of Palestinian territory” while urging the Jewish state to “bring its illegal settlement activity and the occupation to an end”. The draft also issues Labor’s support for the International Court of Justice and the International Criminal Court “and compliance with their binding decisions”, with both courts investigating allegations against Israel of genocide, war crimes and crimes against humanity.
Mr Albanese and Foreign Minister Penny Wong have previously said they support the ICJ when asked if they would arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu – who is subject to a warrant – if he was ever in Australia.
After the Prime Minister used the recent parliamentary fortnight to ram into law reforms to negative gearing and capital gains tax, the latest draft platform has been amended to lock in the backing of unions and the Labor membership to tax hikes unveiled in the May budget. “Labor will limit negative gearing concessions to new housing, return capital gains tax to cost-base indexation, introduce a minimum tax rate on discretionary trusts and provide tax relief for workers,” the latest draft says.
“Labor will deliver a progressive and sustainable tax system, where taxing work doesn’t subsidise the taxing of wealth.” With Jim Chalmers refusing to rule out future tax increases on the gas sector, the draft says a Labor government would ensure Australians “receive a fairer return from their natural resources including through appropriate taxation arrangements”.
The Australian can also reveal there will be a motion pushed at national conference aimed at preventing any Australian parts being used in a global supply chain accessed by Israel’s F-35 bombers if the Jewish state was found by the ICJ to have committed genocide, although it does not mention Israel by name.
The motion to amend the draft platform, sponsored by officials in the Victorian division of the Australian Manufacturing Workers Union, calls on the government to “strengthen defence export controls where there is a risk exports may constitute, enable or assist genocide, war crimes, or crimes against humanity”.
The motion demands a Labor government prevent the Future Fund, superannuation funds and charities from “investing in any entities complicit in illegal settlements or conduct contributing to genocide”. It calls on Labor to “establish enforceable reporting and compliance obligations requiring major institutions and corporations to identify and address genocide risks across their operations and value chains”.
AMWU Victoria assistant secretary Tony Piccolo said the motion would provide transperancy over the outcome of Australia’s defence manufactured products in line with other nations.
“America and England can tell you where their bullets get sold and where they get shot ... Australia can’t,” Mr Piccolo said.
The AI development out of the national platform follows outgoing NSW Labor MP Mark Buttigieg using the party’s state conference on Sunday to warn there would “scarcely be a greater economic and social challenge in the 21st century than the advent of AI”. “We cannot overstate the importance of its introduction and how the human race and, more importantly, as an Australian Labor Party, we deal with it,” he said. “This is a technology that, depending on how we handle it, will either result in massive structural unemployment and a loss of productivity, or a great opportunity to uplift the human race.” Mr Buttigieg warned Labor would lose ground to One Nation if it did not prioritise job creation in the green-energy transition. The document also recommends consideration of an overhaul of the Fair Work Commission in line with calls by the powerful “shoppies” union.
